title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a novel algorithm for constructing PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets within the few-shot meta-learning setting, extending the concept to what the authors term 'meta-PAC' (mPAC). The core contribution is a more stringent and practical guarantee that ensures prediction sets capture uncertainty with high probability not only over future tasks but also specifically for future observations from a new task, conditional on an adaptation set. This addresses a critical need for safety-critical systems where guarantees must hold for individual tasks rather than just on average, differentiating it clearly from prior work like Meta-CP (Meta-Conformal Prediction) which often provides guarantees only on average across tasks. The proposed method, Meta-PS, leverages a two-step calibration process and a meta-learned score function. The authors empirically validate the algorithm's effectiveness and theoretical properties across diverse datasets, including visual (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and medical domains (CDC Heart Dataset), demonstrating that Meta-PS consistently satisfies the mPAC guarantee while producing smaller prediction set sizes compared to other baselines.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

The paper presents a highly original and significant contribution by formalizing and providing an algorithm for 'meta PAC' prediction sets. This mPAC guarantee is a valuable advancement, offering a more robust and actionable form of uncertainty control that holds with high probability over future tasks and adaptation sets, which is crucial for safety-critical applications. The theoretical setting is appealing, and the proposed calibration strategy appears correct and well-justified. The submission is technically sound, building rigorously on established PAC prediction set theory, and Definition 3 and Theorem 1 provide a solid theoretical foundation. The empirical results are strong and extensive, consistently demonstrating that Meta-PS satisfies its stated mPAC guarantee across various datasets and domains, often yielding smaller prediction set sizes compared to baselines. Figure 2 is particularly effective in visually explaining the nuanced differences in guarantees.

Despite these strengths, the paper's presentation and clarity are areas of concern. While the paper is generally well-structured, the notation is quite burdensome and, at times, inconsistent. For instance, the probability parameters (epsilon, alpha, delta) change across sections, which significantly hampers clarity and rigor. A high-level, step-by-step description of the algorithm's objective at the start of Section 4 would greatly improve readability, rather than immediately diving into complex notation. Similarly, the definition of a parametrized prediction set in Section 3.1 is awkwardly phrased and lacks clarity regarding the `f(x,y)` score function and its relation to `F_tau(x)`. Algorithm 1's use of `g(tau, y) := tau * (y=1)` as a score function for `PS-BINOM` is unconventional and needs more rigorous justification or a clearer re-definition of `PS-BINOM`'s expected inputs.

Another weakness lies in the approach of using a single, fixed threshold for all downstream tasks, regardless of the adaptation set size or task variability. In contrast, methods like Meta-CP attempt to estimate a data-dependent threshold, which could be more effective when sufficient few-shot data is available or when tasks exhibit significant variability. This fixed threshold might lead to conservative or less efficient prediction sets in certain scenarios.

From a practical perspective, the computational cost associated with the proposed two-step calibration process, especially with large numbers of calibration tasks (N) and examples per task (n), is a concern. While the theoretical guarantees are strong, the practical scalability in resource-constrained environments needs further consideration. The theoretical foundation, while sound, is noted as a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration), suggesting a potentially modest impact in terms of entirely new methodological breakthroughs. Furthermore, the tightness of Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes is a relevant question for real-world applicability.

The empirical evaluation, while extensive, includes 'PS-Test' as a baseline, which operates under an oracle setup (using 20 shots directly from the test distribution). While informative, this should be explicitly called out as an oracle rather than a practical comparison for meta-learning in the main body or limitations, as it can inflate the perceived performance gap of other methods.

Finally, the paper's engagement with ethical considerations and data provenance, particularly in the checklist and Appendix D.2, exhibits inconsistencies. Claims regarding data licenses, consent, and PII/offensive content are sometimes contradictory or poorly supported by the provided details, suggesting a superficial engagement with these critical aspects. The lack of publicly available code, while promised upon acceptance, is a limitation for thorough review and reproducibility.

**Questions:**

1.  Please standardize the notation for probability parameters (epsilon, alpha, delta) consistently throughout the entire manuscript, including the abstract, definitions, algorithm, and experimental descriptions, for improved clarity and rigor.
2.  In Section 3.1, please clarify the definition of the prediction set `F_tau(x)`. Specifically, explicitly state how `F_tau(x)` is constructed from the score function `f(x,y)` and the parameter `tau`.
3.  In Algorithm 1, explain more rigorously the role and definition of the score function `g` used in `PS-BINOM(S0, g, alpha/2, delta)`. If `g` simply returns `tau_i`, clarify how `PS-BINOM` (which typically takes `(x,y)` pairs) operates on this single scalar `tau_i` and a dummy label `1`. This is a critical technical detail.
4.  What are the specific theoretical requirements or assumptions on the 'meta-learned and potentially adaptable score function' (f) for the mPAC guarantee to hold? Does it need to satisfy any form of initial calibration or performance criteria?
5.  Could the authors provide a brief discussion on the computational complexity and practical runtime of Meta-PS compared to the baselines, especially considering the N applications of `PS-BINOM`? How does this scale with N and n?
6.  Regarding the acknowledged limitation on calibration data, could the authors provide more specific guidance or theoretical insights on the sample complexity (i.e., how N and n scale) required to achieve specific (epsilon, alpha, beta) values in the mPAC setting? An empirical sensitivity analysis or ablation study on `n` (calibration examples per task) would also be valuable.
7.  In the experiments, it seems that calibration uses tasks sampled from N choose k base calibration classes, and testing uses N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Please clarify if samples from the second are truly i.i.d. with respect to the first set of tasks.
8.  Could the authors elaborate on the empirical failure of Meta-CP to control error over the randomness due to the test distribution (as shown in Figures 3c and 6)? A more detailed explanation of why Meta-CP diverges empirically from its stated PAC property (from [14]) in this specific experimental setup would be beneficial.
9.  How sensitive are the empirical results, particularly the prediction set size and observed coverage, to the specific choices of the parameters epsilon, alpha, and beta? Are there practical recommendations or heuristics for setting these values?
10. Consider an ablation study on the number of adaptation shots (`t`) to further demonstrate the 'few-shot' nature and robustness of the method as `t` changes.
11. Revisit the ethical checklist responses (4b, 4d, 4e) and ensure they accurately reflect the content of Appendix D.2 without contradiction or misrepresentation. If licenses are not explicit, state that clearly in the checklist. If consent was not obtained but justified, clarify that distinction.
12. Clarify the subtle notational distinction between `p_theta_i` as a distribution and `theta_i` as a parameter of that distribution (Section 3.2, 4.1).

**Limitations:**

The authors adequately addressed several limitations, including the necessity for sufficient calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee and potential negative societal impacts if the algorithm's assumptions are misunderstood. It would be beneficial to expand on the concluding remark that this algorithm is not a cure-all, emphasizing its reliance on strong assumptions and that guarantees hold on average across new tasks. Further discussion on the computational cost and scalability, especially with large N and n, would enhance the paper's practical utility. The robustness of the guarantee to minor violations of the i.i.d. task assumption in real-world scenarios could also be explored. The paper's effectiveness is inherently tied to the quality and adaptability of the underlying meta-learned score function, a dependency that could be further investigated. The ethical checklist inconsistencies, as noted in the questions, are a significant concern that needs to be addressed. Finally, the reliance on an oracle baseline (PS-Test) in experiments should be explicitly called out as such in the limitations section, and providing publicly available code for reproducibility is essential.

**Rating:**

6: Weak Accept: The paper presents a technically solid and moderately impactful contribution by introducing a novel meta-PAC guarantee that is highly relevant for safety-critical applications in meta-learning. The theoretical setting is appealing, and the empirical results are strong. However, the clarity of the presentation, particularly concerning notation and algorithm details, requires significant improvement. Practical considerations regarding computational cost and the fixed nature of the prediction threshold are also noted. Furthermore, inconsistencies in the ethical checklist and the absence of publicly available code are areas of concern. These issues, while not fundamentally undermining the core contribution, prevent a higher rating and necessitate revisions for acceptance.

Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces Meta-PS, a novel algorithm for constructing PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The core contribution is an extension of the traditional PAC guarantee, termed 'meta-PAC' (mPAC), which ensures that prediction sets capture uncertainty with high probability not only over future tasks but also specifically for future observations from a new task, conditional on an adaptation set. This addresses a critical need for robust uncertainty quantification in meta-learning, particularly for safety-critical systems where guarantees must hold for individual tasks rather than just on average. The proposed algorithm leverages a two-step calibration process and a meta-learned score function to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments across four diverse datasets (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, CDC Heart Dataset) from visual, language, and medical domains demonstrate the effectiveness of Meta-PS, showing it consistently satisfies the mPAC guarantee while producing smaller prediction set sizes compared to other baselines.

**Strengths:**
*   **Originality and Significance**: The paper presents a highly original and significant contribution by extending PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning setting with a novel 'meta-PAC' (mPAC) guarantee. This guarantee is more stringent and practically relevant than existing meta-conformal prediction approaches, as it holds with high probability over future tasks and adaptation sets, which is crucial for safety-critical applications. The distinction from prior work is clearly articulated, particularly in Figure 2, highlighting a valuable advancement in robust uncertainty quantification for meta-learning. The approach of constructing a PAC prediction set over prediction set parameters is novel and well-justified.
*   **Technical Soundness**: The proposed method is reasonable with a solid theoretical analysis. Definition 3 and Theorem 1 provide a rigorous foundation for the mPAC guarantee, and the proposed Algorithm 1 logically follows from this definition. The claims are well-supported by extensive experimental results across diverse datasets and domains (visual, language, medical), comparing Meta-PS against relevant baselines and demonstrating its efficacy in satisfying the mPAC guarantee while producing smaller prediction set sizes.
*   **Clarity and Organization**: The paper is generally well-written and organized. The introduction clearly motivates the problem, and the background sections provide necessary context. Figure 2 is particularly effective in visually explaining the differences in guarantees. The algorithm description and experimental setup are clear, contributing to the paper's overall readability.

**Weaknesses:**
*   **Scope and Generalizability**: The current method seems primarily applicable to metric-based meta-learning methods (e.g., prototypical networks). It would significantly strengthen the paper if the methods could also be applied to optimization-based approaches, such as Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML). Furthermore, for reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, it would be beneficial to explore whether adapting the meta prediction set parameters themselves could yield further improvements.
*   **Experimental Scope and Baselines**: While experiments are conducted on various datasets, the evaluation for mini-ImageNet datasets is limited to 5-way classification. Comparing against more challenging 10-way or 20-way classification problems would provide a more comprehensive assessment. Additionally, the 'PS-Test' baseline, which uses 20 shots directly from the test distribution, represents an oracle setup rather than a practical comparison for meta-learning and should be explicitly called out as such in the discussion of results.
*   **Computational Considerations**: The paper lacks a thorough discussion of the computational cost associated with the proposed two-step calibration process, especially with large N (number of calibration tasks) and n (calibration examples per task). While theoretical guarantees are strong, practical scalability in resource-constrained environments could be a concern. An analysis or empirical comparison of runtime would be valuable.
*   **Clarity and Notational Precision**: The clarity of the paper is significantly hampered by several notational inconsistencies and imprecise language. Specifically, the notation for probability parameters (e.g., 'delta', 'alpha', 'epsilon') changes across sections, which is confusing and indicative of a lack of meticulousness. The definition of a parametrized prediction set in Section 3.1 is awkwardly phrased and lacks clarity regarding the `f(x,y)` score function and its relation to `F_tau(x)`. Algorithm 1's use of `g(tau, y) := tau * (y=1)` as a score function for `PS-BINOM` is unconventional and requires more rigorous justification or a clearer re-definition of `PS-BINOM`'s expected inputs. Minor grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, and run-on sentences also detract from overall readability.
*   **Theoretical Nuances and Practical Guidance**: The paper could benefit from more specific guidance or theoretical insights on the sample complexity (i.e., how N and n scale) required to achieve specific (epsilon, alpha, beta) values in the mPAC setting. Additionally, the specific theoretical requirements or assumptions on the 'meta-learned and potentially adaptable score function (f)' for the mPAC guarantee to hold need to be explicitly stated. A discussion of the robustness of the guarantee to minor violations of the i.i.d. task assumption in real-world scenarios would also enhance practical utility.

**Questions:**
1.  **Scope and Generalizability**:
    *   Could the authors elaborate on whether adapting the meta prediction set parameters themselves (instead of reusing the same ones after adaptation) could lead to further improvements in performance or tighter guarantees?
    *   Can the proposed methods be extended to optimization-based meta-learning approaches, such as Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), beyond the current metric-based methods?
2.  **Experimental Evaluation**:
    *   For the mini-ImageNet dataset, could the authors provide comparisons on more challenging N-way classification problems (e.g., 10-way or 20-way) to provide a more comprehensive evaluation?
    *   An ablation study on the number of adaptation shots (`t`) would be valuable to further demonstrate the 'few-shot' nature and robustness. How does the empirical coverage and prediction set size vary as `t` changes?
    *   Could the authors elaborate on the empirical failure of Meta-CP to control error over the randomness due to the test distribution (as shown in Figures 3c and 6)? A more detailed explanation of why Meta-CP diverges empirically from its stated PAC property (from [14]) in this specific experimental setup would be beneficial.
3.  **Computational Considerations**:
    *   What is the computational complexity of Meta-PS compared to the baselines, particularly considering the two-step calibration process involving N calibration tasks? Could the authors provide an analysis or empirical comparison of runtime, especially how it scales with N and n?
4.  **Theoretical Nuances and Practical Guidance**:
    *   What are the specific theoretical requirements or assumptions on the 'meta-learned and potentially adaptable score function' (`f`) for the mPAC guarantee to hold? Does it need to satisfy any form of initial calibration or performance criteria?
    *   Regarding the acknowledged limitation on calibration data, could the authors provide more specific guidance or theoretical insights on the sample complexity (i.e., how N and n scale) required to achieve specific (epsilon, alpha, beta) values in the mPAC setting, potentially extending the analysis from [3]?
    *   While the i.i.d. task assumption is standard, real-world meta-learning scenarios might involve some degree of task distribution shift. How robust is the proposed mPAC guarantee to minor deviations from the i.i.d. task assumption? Are there conditions under which the guarantee might become overly conservative or fail?
    *   How sensitive are the empirical results, particularly the prediction set size and observed coverage, to the specific choices of the parameters epsilon, alpha, and beta? Are there practical recommendations or heuristics for setting these values?
5.  **Clarity and Notational Precision**:
    *   Please standardize the notation for probability parameters (epsilon, alpha, delta) consistently throughout the entire manuscript, including the abstract, definitions, algorithm, and experimental descriptions. This is paramount for clarity and rigor.
    *   In Section 3.1, please clarify the definition of the prediction set `F_tau(x)`. Specifically, explicitly state how `F_tau(x)` is constructed from the score function `f(x,y)` and the parameter `tau`.
    *   In Algorithm 1, Line 5, the score function `g` is defined as `g(τ, y) := τ * I(y=1)`. Please provide a more rigorous explanation of the role and definition of this `g` function used in `PS-BINOM`, and clarify how `PS-BINOM` (which typically takes `(x,y)` pairs) operates on this single scalar `tau_i` and a dummy label.
    *   Clarify the subtle notational distinction between `p_theta_i` as a distribution and `theta_i` as a parameter of that distribution (Section 3.2, 4.1). Ensure consistency.
    *   Address the grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, and run-on sentences identified throughout the text to improve overall clarity and readability.

**Limitations:**
The authors have generally addressed several limitations of their work, explicitly stating that the approach 'requires enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee,' which is a valid constraint inherent to PAC-style methods. They also discuss potential negative societal impacts, noting that misuse of the guarantee without proper understanding of its assumptions is a risk.

However, several critical limitations warrant further attention:
*   **Computational Cost**: The paper could more thoroughly discuss the computational cost associated with the proposed two-step calibration process, especially with large numbers of calibration tasks (N) and examples per task (n).
*   **Reproducibility and Code Availability**: The lack of publicly available code for reproducibility is a significant limitation for a research paper, although the authors state it will be released upon acceptance.
*   **Ethical and Data Provenance**: The authors' checklist claims (4b, 4d, 4e) regarding data licenses, consent, and PII/offensive content appear to be contradicted or poorly supported by the details provided in Appendix D.2. For instance, stating that licenses for mini-ImageNet and FewRel are 'not explicitly stated' directly contradicts a 'Yes' answer to 'Did you mention the license of the assets?'. This suggests a superficial engagement with ethical considerations and data provenance.
*   **Oracle Baseline**: The reliance on an oracle baseline (PS-Test) in experiments, while informative for theoretical comparison, should be explicitly called out as such in the limitations section, as it inflates the perceived performance gap of other methods and is not a practical comparison for meta-learning.
*   **Practical Guidance**: The paper lacks detailed guidance or theoretical bounds on the sample complexity (N and n) specifically for the mPAC setting, which would be highly valuable for practical deployment. Furthermore, the effectiveness of Meta-PS is inherently tied to the quality and adaptability of the underlying meta-learned score function; this dependency could be further explored in limitations.

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel algorithm for constructing PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The core contribution is an extension of the traditional PAC guarantee, termed 'meta-PAC' (mPAC, Definition 3), which ensures that prediction sets capture uncertainty with high probability not only over future tasks but also specifically for future observations from a new task, conditional on an adaptation set. This addresses a critical need for safety-critical systems where guarantees must hold for individual tasks rather than just on average. The proposed algorithm, Meta-PS (Algorithm 1), leverages a two-step calibration process to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimators/thresholds on each of N distributions, and then uses these to construct a new sample to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC, ultimately generating an $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ with probability $1-\delta$. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC. Empirical results demonstrate the efficacy of Meta-PS across diverse datasets from visual, language, and medical domains, consistently satisfying the meta-PAC guarantee while producing smaller prediction set sizes compared to other baselines.

**Strengths And Weaknesses:**

**Originality:**
This paper presents a highly original contribution by extending PAC prediction sets from previous studies to the meta-learning setting with a novel 'meta-PAC' guarantee. This guarantee is more stringent and practical than existing meta-conformal prediction approaches, as it holds with high probability over future tasks and adaptation sets, which is crucial for safety-critical applications. The concept provides fine-grained control over uncertainty quantification across multiple sources of randomness in meta-learning. The distinction from prior work is clearly articulated, particularly in Figure 2, which effectively visually explains the differences in guarantees.

**Quality:**
The submission is technically sound, building on established PAC prediction set theory and extending it rigorously. Definition 3 and Theorem 1 provide a solid theoretical foundation for the proposed mPAC guarantee, and the proposed Algorithm 1 logically follows from this definition. The claims are well-supported by extensive experimental results across diverse datasets and domains, comparing Meta-PS against relevant baselines, including a strong oracle (PS-Test) and the most direct competitor (Meta-CP). The authors are careful and honest in evaluating both strengths and limitations. However, the technical quality suffers from some inconsistencies and imprecisions in presentation. The notation for probability parameters (e.g., 'delta', 'alpha', 'epsilon') changes across sections, which is confusing and indicative of a lack of meticulousness. Additionally, Algorithm 1's use of `g(tau, y) := tau * I(y=1)` as a score function for `PS-BINOM` is unconventional and needs more rigorous justification or a clearer re-definition of `PS-BINOM`'s expected inputs.

**Clarity:**
This paper is generally well-written and organized, making it easy to follow. The introduction clearly motivates the problem, and the background sections provide necessary context. Figure 2 is particularly effective in visually explaining the differences in guarantees, contributing to the paper's overall readability. However, the clarity is somewhat hampered by the aforementioned notational inconsistencies and imprecise language. The definition of a parametrized prediction set in Section 3.1 is awkwardly phrased and lacks clarity regarding the `f(x,y)` score function and its relation to `F_tau(x)`. Furthermore, some sentences are grammatically awkward or contain run-on clauses, which could be improved for overall readability.

**Significance:**
The results are highly significant. Providing robust uncertainty quantification with strong theoretical guarantees in meta-learning is a critical challenge, especially for real-world deployment in safety-critical systems. The mPAC guarantee advances the state of the art by offering a more reliable form of uncertainty quantification by conditioning correctness on individual future tasks and their adaptation sets. This makes the work a demonstrable advance in the state of the art for meta-learning in sensitive domains, and researchers and practitioners working on robust and trustworthy AI will likely find these ideas valuable and build upon them.

**Questions/Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Computational Cost and Efficiency:** Algorithm 2 involves a grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such a grid search? More broadly, a thorough discussion on the computational cost associated with the proposed two-step calibration process is warranted, especially with large N (number of calibration tasks) and n (calibration examples per task). This includes the cumulative cost for N applications of the base PAC prediction set algorithm (e.g., PS-BINOM) and how it scales with N and n.
2.  **Sample Complexity and Sensitivity:** Regarding the acknowledged need for 'enough calibration datapoints,' the authors should provide more specific guidance or an empirical sensitivity analysis on how the choice of 'n' (calibration examples per task) and 'N' (number of calibration tasks) impacts the tightness of the prediction sets and the empirical satisfaction of the mPAC guarantee. Theoretical insights or bounds on sample complexity would be highly valuable for practical deployment.
3.  **Clarification of Algorithm Details:** In Section 3.1, please clarify the definition of the prediction set `F_tau(x)`, explicitly stating how it is constructed from the score function `f(x,y)` and the parameter `tau`. In Algorithm 1, Line 5, the score function `g(τ, y) := τ * I(y=1)` for `PS-BINOM` needs more rigorous explanation. If `g` simply returns `tau_i`, clarify how `PS-BINOM` (which typically takes `(x,y)` pairs) operates on this single scalar `tau_i` and a dummy label `1`.
4.  **Robustness to Assumptions:** While the i.i.d. task assumption is standard, a brief discussion of the robustness of the proposed mPAC guarantee to minor deviations from this assumption in real-world scenarios could enhance the paper's practical utility.
5.  **Ablation Studies:** To further demonstrate the 'few-shot' nature and robustness, an ablation study on `t` (adaptation shots) would be valuable, showing how empirical coverage and prediction set size vary as `t` changes. Additionally, exploring the sensitivity of empirical results to the specific choices of $\varepsilon, \alpha,$ and $\delta$ would be beneficial.
6.  **Meta-learned Score Function:** What are the specific theoretical requirements or assumptions on the 'meta-learned and potentially adaptable score function' (`f`) for the mPAC guarantee to hold? Does it need to satisfy any form of initial calibration or performance criteria?
7.  **Notational Consistency and Grammar:** Please standardize the notation for probability parameters (epsilon, alpha, delta) consistently throughout the entire manuscript. Additionally, address the grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, and run-on sentences identified throughout the text to improve overall clarity and readability.

**Limitations:**
While the authors adequately acknowledge that the approach requires enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee and briefly discuss potential negative societal impact (elaborated in Appendix F), some areas could be strengthened. The paper's engagement with ethical considerations and data provenance could be more thorough, particularly regarding the consistency between the ethical checklist responses (4b, 4d, 4e) and the details provided in Appendix D.2. For instance, clarifying data licenses and consent procedures more precisely would be beneficial. The reliance on an oracle baseline (PS-Test) in experiments, while informative, should be explicitly called out as such in the limitations section, as it represents an ideal scenario rather than a practical comparison for meta-learning. Finally, the lack of publicly available code for reproducibility is a current limitation for a thorough review, even if it is promised upon acceptance.

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations. The paper presents a highly original and significant contribution, but could benefit from further refinement in clarity, notational consistency, and more detailed discussion of practical aspects like computational cost and sample complexity. Addressing the highlighted questions would substantially improve the manuscript.

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**

This paper introduces Meta-PS, a novel algorithm that extends distribution-free uncertainty quantification to the meta-learning setting, specifically through a "meta-PAC" (mPAC) guarantee. This guarantee is a triply-nested one, ensuring that prediction sets maintain correctness with high probability not only over calibration data but also over future tasks and their respective adaptation sets. This addresses a critical need for safety-critical systems by providing a more stringent and practical guarantee than existing meta-conformal prediction approaches, which often provide guarantees only on average across tasks. The proposed algorithm leverages a two-step calibration process based on binomial concentration and a meta-learned score function. The authors demonstrate the efficacy of Meta-PS across four diverse datasets (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, CDC Heart Dataset) from visual, language, and medical domains, showing that it consistently satisfies the mPAC guarantee while producing competitive or smaller prediction set sizes compared to baselines. The paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value as a straightforward extension of existing results, and contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning, notably conditioning on tasks in a way slightly different from existing work by Adam Fisch et al.

**Strengths:**

The paper presents a technically sound and rigorously evaluated contribution. The proofs appear correct, and the related work is appropriately cited (with the exception of the attribution issue discussed below). The core theoretical results, particularly Definition 3 and Theorem 1, provide a solid foundation for the proposed mPAC guarantee, which is a highly original and significant extension of PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning setting. This more stringent guarantee, holding with high probability over future tasks and adaptation sets, is crucial for real-world deployment in safety-critical systems, advancing the state of the art in robust uncertainty quantification. The experimental section is comprehensive, demonstrating the efficacy of Meta-PS across diverse datasets and domains, and consistently showing that the algorithm satisfies its stated mPAC guarantee while yielding smaller prediction set sizes compared to other baselines that also satisfy a form of correctness guarantee. The approach of constructing a PAC prediction set over prediction set parameters is novel and well-justified. The visual explanation of different guarantees in Figure 2 is particularly effective in clarifying the paper's contribution.

**Weaknesses:**

While the paper presents valuable contributions, several areas require improvement in clarity, technical precision, and comprehensive self-assessment:

1.  **Complexity and Interpretability of Guarantee:** The guarantee provided in this paper is highly complicated to interpret. Even a standard tolerance interval is challenging, and adding an extra parameter incurs a significant cognitive cost for the user, potentially hindering practical adoption.
2.  **Clarity and Readability:** Despite attempts at clarity (e.g., Figure 2 is helpful in concept), the paper's writing can be dense and exceedingly complicated in parts. For example, Figure 2 includes extraneous information and missing explanations (e.g., orientation of stripes, "N" box). Section 4.1 is particularly difficult to follow, suggesting that only a very small community working on PAC-type guarantees will fully understand it. This is exacerbated by notational inconsistencies for probability parameters (e.g., `delta`, `alpha`, `epsilon`) across different sections, which significantly hampers rigor and clarity. Several sentences are grammatically awkward or contain run-on clauses, further impacting readability.
3.  **Technical Novelty and Interest:** While the algorithm for chaining concentration arguments twice is interesting and clever, the underlying technical result is largely a straightforward extension of existing results. The domain area itself, applying conformal prediction to meta-learning, is not entirely novel, given prior work by Adam Fisch et al. The core idea is essentially applying binomial concentration twice instead of once to condition further on variables.
4.  **Attribution and Terminology:** There is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as "PAC prediction sets" and citing both Vovk and [3] as the source is inaccurate; Vovk should receive sole credit for inventing this idea. Similarly, the so-called "PS-Binom" algorithm is essentially the same as Vovk's Proposition 2b in [21]. While [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea. The rebranding as "PAC prediction sets" instead of "training-conditional validity" is also problematic, as they are equivalent, and the latter terminology predates the former. This lack of meticulousness in attribution is a serious concern.
5.  **Computational Cost and Scalability:** The paper lacks a thorough discussion of the computational cost associated with the proposed two-step calibration process. Algorithm 1 requires N applications of the base PAC prediction set algorithm (PS-BINOM), where N can be large (e.g., 500 in experiments). While the base algorithm is efficient, the cumulative cost for very large N and n (calibration samples per task) could be a significant practical concern for deployment in resource-constrained environments.
6.  **Sample Complexity Guidance:** While acknowledging that the approach requires enough calibration datapoints, the paper does not provide detailed guidance or theoretical insights on the sample complexity (i.e., how N and n scale) required to achieve specific (epsilon, alpha, beta) values in the mPAC setting. This would be highly valuable for practical deployment and understanding trade-offs.
7.  **Ethical Checklist Inconsistencies and Reproducibility:** The authors' checklist claims regarding data licenses, consent, and PII/offensive content are directly contradicted or poorly supported by details in Appendix D.2. For example, stating that licenses for mini-ImageNet and FewRel are "not explicitly stated" directly contradicts a "Yes" answer to "Did you mention the license of the assets?". Such inconsistencies suggest a superficial engagement with ethical considerations and data provenance. Furthermore, the lack of publicly available code for reproducibility is a significant limitation for a research paper, despite the checklist promising release upon acceptance.
8.  **Oracle Baseline:** The "PS-Test" baseline, which uses 20 shots directly from the test distribution, represents an oracle setup rather than a practical comparison for meta-learning. While informative, this should be explicitly called out as such in the limitations section, as it can inflate the perceived performance gap of other methods.
9.  **Assumptions on Meta-Learned Score Function:** The effectiveness of Meta-PS is inherently tied to the quality and adaptability of the underlying meta-learned score function (`f`). The paper assumes a "given" function, but the practical performance will heavily depend on this pre-trained component. More specific theoretical requirements or assumptions on this function for the mPAC guarantee to hold would be beneficial.

**Questions for Authors:**

1.  What does it mean for tasks to be "iid" in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold, and how robust is the mPAC guarantee to minor deviations from this i.i.d. task assumption?
2.  Please standardize the notation for probability parameters (epsilon, alpha, delta) consistently throughout the entire manuscript, including the abstract, definitions, algorithm, and experimental descriptions.
3.  Could the authors provide a brief discussion on the computational complexity and practical runtime of Meta-PS, especially considering the N applications of `PS-BINOM`? How does this scale with N and n compared to baselines?
4.  Regarding the acknowledged limitation on calibration data, could the authors provide more detailed guidance or theoretical insights on the sample complexity (i.e., how N and n scale) required to achieve specific (epsilon, alpha, beta) values in the mPAC setting, potentially extending the analysis from [3]?
5.  In Algorithm 1, Line 5, the score function `g` is defined as `g(τ, y) := τ * I(y=1)`. For clarity, could the description of Algorithm 1 or Section 4.2 explicitly state that this step effectively finds a quantile of the `τ_i` values, and explain the role of the 'dummy label 1' more directly? A more rigorous explanation of how `PS-BINOM` operates on this single scalar `tau_i` and a dummy label `1` is needed.
6.  Revisit the ethical checklist responses (4b, 4d, 4e) and ensure they accurately reflect the content of Appendix D.2 without contradiction or misrepresentation. If licenses are not explicit, state that clearly in the checklist. If consent was not obtained but justified, clarify that distinction.
7.  Provide the code and detailed instructions for reproducibility. While the checklist states it will be released upon acceptance, access to the code is essential for verifying experimental claims and implementation details.
8.  Could the authors elaborate on the empirical failure of Meta-CP to control error over the randomness due to the test distribution (as shown in Figures 3c and 6)? A more detailed explanation of why Meta-CP diverges empirically from its stated PAC property (from [14]) in this specific experimental setup would be beneficial.
9.  How sensitive are the empirical results, particularly the prediction set size and observed coverage, to the specific choices of the parameters epsilon, alpha, and beta? Are there practical recommendations or heuristics for setting these values?

**Limitations:**

The authors have addressed some limitations, particularly regarding the inherent requirement for sufficient calibration datapoints. However, as noted in the weaknesses, further discussion on the practical computational cost, detailed sample complexity guidance, the role of the oracle baseline (PS-Test), and a more robust engagement with ethical considerations and data provenance (including ensuring consistency in checklist responses) would significantly strengthen the paper. The dependence on the quality of the meta-learned score function also warrants further exploration.

**Rating:** 6: Weak Accept

**Justification:**

The paper presents technically correct proofs and related work (with the critical exception of the attribution issue), introducing a novel two-stage training-conditional guarantee for meta-learning that has some value as an extension of existing results. The comprehensive experiments support the claims. However, the paper suffers from significant clarity issues, including dense writing, confusing notation, and an overly complicated guarantee interpretation. The major attribution problem regarding Vovk's work is a serious concern that must be fully amended for acceptance. While the core idea is clever, its novelty is tempered by being an extension of existing results. Despite these drawbacks, the contribution of a more stringent mPAC guarantee for uncertainty quantification in safety-critical meta-learning applications is valuable. Addressing the identified weaknesses, particularly the attribution, clarity, and ethical checklist inconsistencies, is crucial for improving the paper's overall quality and impact.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Weaknesses:

2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
